title: Today's AI Brief: Former Google and Apple Researchers Launch a Startup to Build AI’s Missing Feedback Loop
summary: A new startup from ex-Google and Apple researchers aims to improve AI feedback loops, while YouTube enhances AI labeling and identification.
imageAlt: Image from source article for Former Google and Apple Researchers Launch a Startup to Build AI’s Missing Feedback Loop
imageCaption: Former Google and Apple Researchers Launch a Startup to Build AI’s Missing Feedback Loop
imageSourceTitle: Former Google and Apple Researchers Launch a Startup to Build AI’s Missing Feedback Loop

body.text: I'm Leo Stone, and welcome to today's AI Brief. We're looking at some interesting developments in the world of artificial intelligence. A group of researchers who used to work at big names like Google DeepMind, Apple, OpenAI, and Meta Superintelligence Labs have started a new company called Trajectory. Their goal is to create AI systems that get smarter as you use them, which sounds like a no-brainer, but it's actually a pretty tough problem. I mean, who hasn't struggled with an AI assistant that just doesn't get it?

The idea behind Trajectory is to build what's known as a 'feedback loop' into AI systems. This means that as you interact with the AI, it learns from those interactions and gets better over time. It's a bit like how you might adjust your Google search queries based on the results you get - Trajectory wants to make AI do the same thing. This could be a game-changer for AI applications, from chatbots to virtual assistants. Just imagine having an AI that actually gets better at understanding you over time.

Former Google and Apple Researchers Launch a Startup to Build AI’s Missing Feedback Loop is also worth a quick mention. The main content says: A group of AI researchers who previously worked at Google DeepMind, Apple, OpenAI , and Meta Superintelligence Labs announced on Wednesday they’re launching a new startup called Trajectory , which aims to help companies regularly improve their AI products by training on real-world user interactions. YouTube is putting AI labels where you’ll actually see them, from theverge.com, is also worth a quick mention.
